title: Build Your Data Story: The Visualization Checklist from Insight to Persuasion

sections.title: Define Your Data Story's Core Message
sections.prompt: Identify a dataset or analysis you completed recently. In one sentence, write the insight that matters most. Now map it: What's the problem your data solves? What's your key finding? What decision should follow? This is your story's spine—your visualization must support it.

sections.title: Choose Your Plot Type (Because Charts Aren't Neutral)
sections.prompt: Take your insight from above. Sketch how it would look as: (1) a bar chart, (2) a line chart, (3) a scatter plot. For each, note what truth it reveals and what it hides. Which shape tells your story best? Which could mislead? Explain why the right choice matters.

sections.title: Spot and Fix Design Mistakes That Kill Credibility
sections.prompt: Review a visualization you made in the last month. Count the colors (max 5–6?). Check your axis labels (descriptive or vague?). Do you have a cluttered legend? Identify one mistake from the blog that applies to you. Rewrite it—what changes?

sections.title: Code It with Intention: Matplotlib or Seaborn
sections.prompt: Build a simple visualization using either library. Include: descriptive axis labels, intentional color choices (max 5–6), meaningful title. For each design choice, write a comment explaining why you made it. The code should tell the story of your thinking.

sections.title: Test Your Story: Does It Land Without Explanation?
sections.prompt: Share your visualization with someone unfamiliar with the data. Don't explain it. Ask: 'What's the main story?' and 'What question do you have?' If their answer doesn't match your intent, what is the plot hiding or over-emphasizing by accident? How would you fix it?

cta: Rebuild one of your analyses using these principles. Add labels, remove the junk. Share how the visualization changed what people understood about your findings.
